description: Dependency cooldown. Almost every published supply-chain attack is caught and yanked within hours to days, so the cheapest defence is simply not to be first: waiting puts the detection window in front of us instead of behind. Of the ten incidents surveyed in https://blog.yossarian.net/2025/11/21/We-should-all-be-using-dependency-cooldowns, eight had a window under a week and 14 days would have covered all but xz-utils.

This repo is a closer fit for that threat model than most. tj-actions on that list was a compromised GitHub Action that exfiltrated CI secrets, and actions/checkout and actions/setup-node run here in jobs holding FORGE_API_TOKEN. Pinning them by SHA fixes WHICH commit runs; only the cooldown addresses whether that commit has been looked at by anyone yet.

The cost is bounded, and deliberately does not extend to security fixes: Renovate raises those as soon as it detects them, bypassing this cooldown with no configuration needed. That bypass is only as good as the detection behind it, though — Renovate learns a version is vulnerable from GitHub's vulnerability alerts, so if those are ever switched off for this repo, the cooldown keeps applying to security fixes with nothing left to exempt them. Keep them on. npm audit --omit=dev --audit-level=high gates every PR besides, so an unfixed high advisory in a shipped dependency turns the whole queue red until it is resolved.

packageRules.description: Both copies of Mermaid ship in the bundle. Bump them in one PR so the corpus parses them as a pair, rather than leaving one major green while the other is untested.

mermaid-10 is an alias (npm:mermaid@^10.9.3) and it exists to BE the pinned major-10 line — a diagram pinned to 10 must keep rendering as 10. Grouping does not enforce this; only allowedVersions does. Crossing to 11 here would silently defeat the version dropdown.
Pinned exact and deployed with production credentials, so a bump should be a reviewed PR that runs the full gate — never a floating @latest resolved on the runner.

The Forge CLI refuses to run on the 22.23.1 and 24.17.0 builds exactly — its engines range is >=20.18.1 <22.23.1 || >22.23.1 <24.17.0 || >24.17.0. The workflow says so in a comment beside every node-version, but Renovate cannot read comments and proposed 22.23.1 (PR #8), which passed both required checks: the corpus job never invokes the CLI, so nothing that runs on a PR can fail on a Node build the CLI rejects. Only the deploy would have. Stay on 22.x and step over the bad build.
